name: FROM
description: Sets the base image for subsequent instructions. Every valid Dockerfile must start with a FROM instruction. Multi-stage builds can use multiple FROM instructions.
notes: Use a specific tag rather than 'latest' for reproducible builds. The AS name is used in multi-stage builds to copy artifacts between stages.

name: RUN
description: Executes commands in a new layer on top of the current image and commits the results. The resulting committed image is used for the next step in the Dockerfile.
example: RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y curl
notes: Combine multiple commands with && to reduce image layers. Use --mount=type=cache to cache package manager directories.

name: CMD
syntax: CMD ["executable","param1","param2"] | CMD ["param1","param2"] | CMD command param1 param2
description: Provides defaults for an executing container. There can only be one CMD instruction in a Dockerfile; if listed more than once, only the last takes effect.
notes: CMD is overridden by arguments passed to 'docker run'. Use exec form to avoid shell string munging. If ENTRYPOINT is defined, CMD provides default arguments to it.

name: ENTRYPOINT
description: Configures a container to run as an executable. Unlike CMD, ENTRYPOINT is not overridden by docker run arguments; instead those arguments are appended.
notes: Use exec form to ensure the process receives OS signals. Can be overridden with --entrypoint flag. Combine with CMD to provide default but overridable arguments.

name: COPY
description: Copies files or directories from the build context (or a named build stage) into the container filesystem at the specified path.
notes: Preferred over ADD for simple file copies. Use --from=<stage> to copy from a previous build stage. Paths relative to WORKDIR.

name: ADD
description: Copies files, directories, or remote URLs from the source to the container filesystem. Also automatically extracts local tar archives.
notes: Use COPY instead unless you specifically need URL fetching or automatic tar extraction. ADD with a URL does not support authentication.

name: EXPOSE
description: Informs Docker that the container listens on the specified network ports at runtime. It is documentation only and does not actually publish the port.
notes: Does not make ports accessible from the host. Use -p or -P flags with 'docker run' or port mappings in docker-compose to publish ports.

name: ENV
description: Sets environment variables that persist in the container at runtime. These variables are available to all subsequent instructions and to the running container.
notes: Environment variables set with ENV persist in the container. Use ARG for build-time only variables. Can be overridden at runtime with -e flag.

name: ARG
description: Defines a variable that users can pass at build-time to the builder with the --build-arg flag. Unlike ENV, ARG values are not available in the running container.
notes: ARG variables are not persisted into the final image. Pre-defined ARGs like HTTP_PROXY, HTTPS_PROXY are available without declaration. Scope is from definition line to end of build stage.

name: WORKDIR
description: Sets the working directory for any subsequent RUN, CMD, ENTRYPOINT, COPY, and ADD instructions. If the directory does not exist, it is created automatically.
notes: Can be used multiple times. If a relative path is provided, it is relative to the previous WORKDIR. Prefer WORKDIR over RUN cd ... for clarity.

name: USER
description: Sets the user name (or UID) and optionally the user group (or GID) to use for all subsequent RUN, CMD, and ENTRYPOINT instructions.
notes: Best practice for security: don't run containers as root. The user must exist in /etc/passwd or be created with RUN useradd/adduser before this instruction.

name: VOLUME
description: Creates a mount point with the specified name and marks it as holding externally mounted volumes from the native host or other containers.
notes: Any data written to a VOLUME path after this instruction is discarded. Data in volumes is not part of the image and persists across container restarts.

name: LABEL
description: Adds metadata to an image as key-value pairs. Labels are inherited by child images and can be queried with 'docker inspect'.
notes: Use OCI standard label keys where applicable: org.opencontainers.image.title, .version, .description, .source, etc.

name: HEALTHCHECK
description: Tells Docker how to test a container to check that it is still working. Allows Docker to detect containers that are running but not serving correctly.
notes: Options: --interval (default 30s), --timeout (default 30s), --start-period (default 0s), --retries (default 3). Exit code 0 = healthy, 1 = unhealthy.

name: ONBUILD
description: Adds a trigger instruction to the image that executes when the image is used as a base for another build. Useful for base images that need to inject build logic into child images.
example:
ONBUILD COPY . /app/src
ONBUILD RUN npm install
notes: ONBUILD triggers fire immediately after FROM in the child Dockerfile. They are not inherited by grandchild images. Visible in 'docker inspect' output.

name: STOPSIGNAL
description: Sets the system call signal that will be sent to the container to exit. Can be a signal name (SIGTERM) or unsigned number (15).
notes: Default is SIGTERM. Overridable at runtime with --stop-signal. Important for graceful shutdown: ensure your process handles the configured signal.

name: SHELL
description: Overrides the default shell used for the shell form of commands in RUN, CMD, and ENTRYPOINT instructions. Default on Linux is ["/bin/sh", "-c"].
notes: On Windows, default is ["cmd", "/S", "/C"]. Using bash with pipefail ensures non-zero exit codes from piped commands are properly propagated.

name: MAINTAINER
description: Sets the author field of the generated image. This instruction is deprecated in favor of using LABEL with org.opencontainers.image.authors.
notes: Deprecated. Use LABEL maintainer="..." or LABEL org.opencontainers.image.authors="..." instead.

name: image
description: A read-only template containing instructions for creating a Docker container. Built from a Dockerfile and stored in a registry or local cache. Images are layered for efficiency.
example:
docker pull nginx:alpine
docker build -t myapp:1.0 .
notes: Images are immutable. Layers are shared between images to save disk space. Tagged with name:tag format; 'latest' is the default tag.

name: container
description: A runnable instance of a Docker image. Containers are isolated processes with their own filesystem, networking, and process space, created from images.
notes: Containers are ephemeral by default. State is lost when removed unless volumes are used. Managed individually or via orchestrators like Docker Compose or Kubernetes.

name: volume
description: A Docker-managed persistent storage mechanism that exists independently of containers. Volumes survive container removal and can be shared between containers.
example:
docker volume create mydata
docker run -v mydata:/data myapp
notes: Preferred for persisting data. Stored in Docker's storage area (/var/lib/docker/volumes/). Supports volume drivers for remote storage (NFS, cloud, etc.).

name: network
description: A Docker-managed virtual network that enables communication between containers. Docker creates bridge, host, and none networks by default.
example:
docker network create mynet
docker run --network mynet myapp
notes: Containers on the same user-defined network can resolve each other by name. Swarm mode adds overlay networks for cross-host communication.

name: service
description: A Docker Swarm abstraction representing a desired state of a container across a cluster. Services manage replicas, rolling updates, and placement constraints.
notes: Services are a Swarm mode concept. In Docker Compose, 'services' are the building blocks but without Swarm-level scheduling.

name: stack
description: A Docker Swarm object representing a collection of services defined in a Compose file and deployed together as a unit.
notes: Stacks are the Swarm equivalent of Docker Compose applications. They group related services, networks, and volumes for a complete application.

name: secret
description: A Docker Swarm object for securely storing sensitive data such as passwords, TLS certificates, and API keys, available only to services that are explicitly granted access.
notes: Secrets are encrypted at rest and in transit. They are mounted as files in /run/secrets/ inside containers. Available in Swarm mode and Docker Compose v3+.

name: config
description: A Docker Swarm object similar to secrets but for non-sensitive configuration data. Configs are stored in plain text and can be inspected.
notes: Unlike secrets, configs are not encrypted at rest. Use for configuration files that need to be distributed to services but don't contain sensitive data.

name: plugin
description: An extension to Docker that provides additional capabilities such as custom network drivers, volume drivers, or logging drivers.
example:
docker plugin install vieux/sshfs
docker plugin enable vieux/sshfs
notes: Plugins run as privileged containers. Volume plugins allow Docker volumes to be backed by remote storage systems like NFS, S3, or cloud block storage.

name: context
description: A Docker CLI feature for managing connections to different Docker endpoints (local daemon, remote hosts, Kubernetes clusters) and switching between them.
example:
docker context create myremote --docker host=ssh://user@remote
docker context use myremote
notes: Contexts store connection configuration for Docker hosts. Useful for managing multiple environments without changing environment variables.

name: bridge
description: The default network mode. Creates a virtual network interface (docker0) on the host. Containers on the same bridge network can communicate; external access requires port publishing.
notes: The default bridge network lacks automatic DNS resolution between containers. User-defined bridge networks support DNS by container name and are the recommended approach.

name: host
description: Removes network isolation between the container and the Docker host. The container shares the host's network stack directly, with no port translation.
notes: Only available on Linux. Provides best network performance but reduces isolation. Useful for applications needing low latency or that manage their own port binding.

name: none
description: Disables all networking for a container. The container only has a loopback interface and cannot communicate with other containers or the outside world.
notes: Use for maximum isolation of containers that don't need network access, such as batch processing jobs or security-sensitive workloads.

name: overlay
description: Creates a distributed network spanning multiple Docker daemon hosts. Used in Docker Swarm mode to enable communication between services across nodes.
notes: Requires Swarm mode or key-value store. Encrypts traffic between nodes with --opt encrypted. Supports VXLAN encapsulation for cross-host container communication.

name: macvlan
description: Assigns a MAC address to each container, making it appear as a physical device on the network. Allows containers to be directly accessible on the LAN.
example: docker network create --driver macvlan --subnet=192.168.1.0/24 --gateway=192.168.1.1 -o parent=eth0 mymacvlan
notes: Requires the host's NIC to support promiscuous mode. Useful for legacy applications that need a physical network presence. Does not support communication with the Docker host by default.

name: named-volume
description: A Docker-managed volume identified by a name. Docker handles storage location and lifecycle. Named volumes persist after container removal and can be shared between containers.
notes: Preferred for persistent application data. Easier to manage than bind mounts on Windows and Mac (no path translation issues). Can use volume drivers for remote storage.

name: bind-mount
description: Maps a file or directory from the host machine into the container. Changes are immediately reflected on both sides, making it ideal for development.
notes: Performance varies by OS. On Mac/Windows, file system events may not propagate correctly. Use :ro (read-only) suffix for security. Not recommended for production data storage.

name: tmpfs
description: Mounts a temporary filesystem stored in the host's memory. Data is not persisted to disk and is lost when the container stops. Useful for sensitive or ephemeral data.
notes: Only available on Linux. Useful for writing temporary files that should not be persisted (session data, sensitive files). Does not increase image size.
